# Transcrição: 01-Growth vs Scaling

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So now let's talk about scaling and the difference between scaling and growth. Now when people look at Facebook ads, you see so many case studies and insights on how people scale their business through Facebook ads. But here's the problem. We've built some stability. We're doing profitability. Obviously we're following the BPM method here. And then we get into the scaling stage. So we have generated our profitability. We've gone through all of the right steps through the BPM method and now we're increasing our spend and we've gone from $1000 a day to $5000 a day and things are great. You've optimized your conversion rates, your AOV, everything is great and now you want to go for real growth and you want to scale that up and you go from $5,000 to $20,000. Could be over a few days, could be, you know, however we go through that scaling and whatever path you want to take, you've gone from $100 to $1000 to $5000 to $20,000. Now the thing about this is it's not just Facebook ads that you are scaling. So let me just kind of illustrate this with a timeline view now. So let's say day zero, you get started and now you're spending $100 a day and by day 15, you've created stability and profitability. So you're spending $1000 a day two weeks into your new campaign and life's good. You're thinking this is great. I'm following the process. Things are going really well for me and now I want to step up and spend more money. And in the next two weeks, you scale your spend from $1000 to $5000 a day and you know, things are going amazingly well. Like the return on ad spend is good, profitability, etc. Then you push it even harder and by day 50, you're now spending $20,000 a day on a 3x return on ad spend. whether you're actually scaling too fast. The cost of scaling could be huge. ## [06:02 - 09:05]

I didn't have control, although I was still spending 200K a day and we were generating half a million in revenue a day. But we were literally hanging on. We were every day, I particular was logging in every day. The first thing I wanted to do is how were my numbers from last night? I'd be logging in throughout the day, how are my numbers doing, how are my numbers doing? Late at night before I go to bed, I'm logging in how are my numbers doing? Because when you're spending those kind of numbers, and numbers that you're not used to, whether it's the 10,000 a day, 1,000 a day, 50, 100,000 a day, if you're not prepared and you're not ready for all the eventualities and you don't have the controls in place, it is highly stressful as well. How about on the other side about fulfillment? So once you generate the sales, can your fulfillment team deliver? Can you deliver them? How about customer satisfaction? Can you maintain the high standards you've already set on product or service delivery and the after sales care because there will always be inquiries, post purchase, how do you do this? When's my delivery coming? Are you ready for that? And then one of the most basic ones is do you have enough money to pay for the ads? Like when you're scaling up, you need to be aware that if you're paying on credit card, for example, what are your credit card limits? One of the other things that some people have been caught up in is scaling up their ad spend and actually their card gets blocked by the payment provider because it's seen as fraudulent because there's a massive spike in spend where for example, Facebook may have been taking $500 out of your account every three, four days, now it's trying to take $2,000 or $3,000. That's an alert. You can easily mitigate that but it's being able to plan in advance for what could come up and also considering your payment processor. If you're generating $10,000 or revenue a day and then all of a sudden you go to $50,000 or $100,000, that's another flag as well. There's a whole lot of other things like higher refund rates, lower page scores, loss of cash, liquidity and loss of goodwill. There are many things that can go wrong and I want to highlight these to you because so many of these guru posts, these YouTube videos, these postings in different groups hide what really happens when you scale up. I've spoken to a lot of people that have had these big scaling success stories but they suffer either they've burnt out or their satisfaction rates dropped, maybe they've lost their page because the page feedback scores have dropped. ## [09:05 - 12:05]

by the end of month one, we're now up to $1,000 a day. We want to do this because we want to build the systems as we increase our spend. By day 90, this is like three months from launch, we're now spending $5,000 a day but we've built our processes around that. Our website is now faster, it can take more load. Our customer service team has been geared up, we have an extra person helping us out. Our processes have been improved. We're constantly checking on products and service delivery and then by day 180, so six months on from growth, from the start, we're now growing to $20,000 a day in ad spend but we can cope. Our ad account is stable, our payment provider is totally on board, our payment processor has got us in good standing, et cetera, et cetera. You might look at this and say, I don't care, I want to scale as high as I can as fast as possible. That is absolutely fine but bear in mind the impact on your business because if you're in this for the midterm or the long term, you might not have a business left if you scale too fast and you'll know this if you've done this already, that Facebook will throttle your ad spend, they'll cap your ad spend because they don't also want to see this happen because unfortunately, some bad actors will literally launch a new ad account, launch a new website, ramp up their ad spend and this used to happen many years ago, not so much now because Facebook had blocked it and they would burn out their ad accounts, take a lot of money from people, they would not deliver the products and they'd disappear and they'll start a new business and that process would repeat. Some of the blockers that Facebook put in place, maybe it feels like they're slowing down your progress, are done because of the bad actors that are in place. So now let's look at what planned growth might look like. So you're ensuring your testing process is constantly creating new ads, new creators and audiences. It's exactly what the graduation testing process is and actually that came out because of the problems I had in 2015 that I didn't have enough creatives in place when we were scaling. At that time, considering we were doing six figure daily spends, I had a team of four designers working on creatives. So they were constantly churning out new angles, new ideas. So as you grow your spend, you're growing your team, you're growing your capability, we did it all in-house. You might need to outsource some agency or freelancers to start giving you more content, more ideas. Everything breaks when you scale but you couldn't plan ahead if you know what's coming up and that's what I want you to absolutely know. You also want to ensure that you're only scaling your spend within audience size constraints. So as we've been through already, understanding ocean boats and hooks, even though you have a large audience size, doesn't mean that everyone is ready to buy. ## [12:05 - 15:06]

targetable. And that's why we go through the option insights to understand where to find that stability and what we also need to consider. Planned growth includes speed and load testing. So we talk about conversion rate optimization, but how about testing higher traffic on your website? How does the speed get impacted then? So when we do conversion rate optimization, we're looking to speed test based on that moment in time. So you can go to GT metrics or Pingdom or any other site key in your URL. And all it's doing is saying, Hey, right now your page takes three seconds to load. And here's a few things that you can improve. How about you do load testing? And there are tools out there. You just need to search for load testing, which will send 100,000 people concurrently at the same time to your website, which for a lot of sites would probably break them as well. So for example, I've run TV campaigns where we've had a massive, massive spike in traffic, but we planned ahead and we were able to deal with the traffic. If we didn't, then we would have spent, you know, 25, 50 K on a TV ad spot and completely wasted that traffic with a sales funnel or a sales page that can't cope. So you absolutely need to plan ahead. I've been in Econ for a long time now. I've been running digital products and services and lead gen volume is something that most businesses want and need. But just spending the time to prepare ahead of it makes all the difference. Even your team keeping everyone informed. And that means even for example, if you are planning to really ramp up your spend or you've got big promotional campaign coming up, let your customer service team know, let your fulfillment company know whoever it is that you're going to get, potentially get more orders come through, maybe even recruiting ahead of time. So you know, if you talk about the end of the year and Black Friday, you know it's coming. You know it impacts your business. Not planning in June and July for what you need in November, because for example, if you're recruiting and you want to recruit decent talent, it's not about how quickly you can recruit. It's about how quickly they are able to fulfill the role. So for any role, I always give myself three months. So if I'm hiring a customer service rep or a media manager or an account manager or a marketing manager, I'm always looking at a three month window for the point that I get them into the business to where I believe they'll be fully, fully up to speed and 100% in control. Now obviously some people get there faster, but most people need to get there within three months. Then you look at the hiring time as well. Yes, there are kind of differences in between that base and role, but essentially you have to prepare for the worst case scenario when you're scaling and looking to grow your company as well. ## [18:08 - 19:25]

impacted by me spending money on my ads. For example, my website, my customer service, my fulfillment, etc. And then say, right, what would be the scenario if I had 10x my spend, 100x my spend, what would be the impact on these different areas? Then you can note them down and manage your risk. Maybe you say, right, customer services, we don't really get many messages. And if we 10x our spend, we get 10x more sales, we go from, you know, five emails a day to 50. Yeah, we can cope with that. That's fine. That's absolutely fine. And you can mitigate and manage your risks. But when you go into growth mode, that's how brands are created, because brands create experiences that people enjoy. People want to interact and learn from the brand, find out more about what they're about, because they have had a great experience, not just in the ad and the sales process, but also post purchase. They want to buy from you again. They want to tell their friends about you. That's a great brand experience. And that's where true growth comes from. That's where lifetime value is built and customer loyalty. Then you talk about sustainable revenue and profit growth that comes through planning as well. It's actually less stressful and creates more predictability. And yes, you may lower your potential revenue and profit in the short term, but that's a decision you need to make as to what's important to you right now.
